key_insight: An LLM can translate natural-language business context into structured hub capacity adjustments, then refine decisions through optimization feedback.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical way to inject qualitative operational knowledge into transportation planning models where purely numerical optimization misses real-world context.
method: LLM parses textual business inputs into a decision table.
Each contextual item is mapped to capacity change direction and magnitude.
An optimization model validates candidate decisions using routing performance metrics in an iterative loop.
evidence_signal: On a real-world 13-hub freight network in the southeastern US, the method reached a 2.8% optimality gap versus 11.0% for the baseline without textual inputs.
watch_item: Performance depends on reliable LLM reasoning and well-structured business text, so inconsistent prompts or weak validation could lead to unstable recommendations.
paper_title: LLM-Guided Transportation Hub Capacity Planning with Textual Business Inputs
